prompt: Flip a fair coin 10 times. What is the probability (in %) that you'll see at least one streak of 3 or more heads in a row?
source: Standard tribonacci-recurrence derivation. Discussed in Mlodinow, The Drunkard's Walk (2008), ch. 9, and Diaconis & Mosteller, 'Methods for Studying Coincidences', JASA 1989.
revealHeadline: Streaks are more common than they feel.
revealExplanation: When people are asked to write down a ‘random’ sequence of 10 flips, they almost always avoid runs of 3 or more. Real random sequences contain runs that often. Just 10 flips already cross the 50/50 line, which is why fake-randomness studies (Diaconis, Mlodinow) can distinguish people's guesses from real coin data with high accuracy.
revealWorked: Let f(n) count binary sequences of length n with NO HHH substring. The tribonacci recurrence f(n) = f(n−1) + f(n−2) + f(n−3) with f(0)=1, f(1)=2, f(2)=4 gives f(10) = 504. So P(no HHH in 10 flips) = 504 / 2^10 = 504/1024, and P(at least one HHH) = 1 − 504/1024 = 520/1024 ≈ 0.508.
